It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Honden File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Seven mysteries of Echigo]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Kota-jinja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/8439 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://joetsukankonavi.jp/spot.php?id=166 Jōetsu tourist information] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Niigata Prefecture]] [[Category:Jōetsu, Niigata]] [[Category:Echigo Province]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6 4]][[en:Kota Shrine (Niigata)]]]] [[Category:ddk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6 5]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kota Shrine (Niigata)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Kota Shrine (Niigata)]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:mmm]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kota Shrine (Niigata)]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Kota Shrine (Niigata)]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Honden File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) shaden.JPG|Shaden File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Torii ==See also== * [[Ichinomiya]] * [[List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] * [[Seven mysteries of Echigo]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== *{{commons Category-inline|Kota-jinja}} *[https://niigata-kankou.or.jp/spot/8439 Niigata Prefecture Kanko Navi] {{in Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. [[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buqqhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buqqhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buqqhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buqqhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku {{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]

It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buqqhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buqqhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== {{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 8]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Kota Shrine | native_name = 居多神社 | image = Kota Shrine and Shinran.JPG | caption = Kota Shrine with statue of Shinran to right {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Niigata Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|37|9|59.32|N|138|13|23.50|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = Kota Jinja | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Ichinomiya]] | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]]
[[Nunakawahime]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = 1-11 Gochi 6-chōme, Jōetsu-shi, Niigata-ken 942-0081 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = May 3 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Kota Shrine'''|居多神社|Kota-jinja}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the Gochi neighborhood of the city of [[Jōetsu, Niigata|Jōetsu]], [[Niigata Prefecture]]. It is one of the three shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Echigo Province]]. Kotodamaya.com, [http://www.kotodamaya.com/kota-jinja/ "Kota Jinja"]; retrieved 2012-10-24.{{cite encyclopedia|encyclopedia=Kokushi Daijiten |title=居多神社 |url=http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |accessdate=2012-10-26 |year=2012 |publisher=Shogakukan |location=Tokyo |language=Japanese |trans-title=Kota Shrine |oclc=683276033 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070825113418/http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ |archivedate=2007-08-25 }} The main festival of the shrine is held annually on May 3.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Kota Jinja are: * {{nihongo|[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi no mikoto]]|大国主命||}} * {{nihongo|Nunagawahime no mikoto|奴奈川姫命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Takeminakata|Takeminakata no mikoto]]|建御名方命||}} * {{nihongo|[[Kotoshironushi|Kotoshironushi no mikoto]]|事代主命||}} ==History== The origins of Kota Shrine is unknown. Similarly named shrines, such as the [[Keta Taisha]] in [[Noto Province]], are distributed in a relatively small area along the [[Sea of Japan]] coastline of the [[Hokuriku region]] and all are believed to have a connection with migrants from the [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] area in the [[Kofun period]]. The first known documentary mention of Kota Shrine is in the ''[[Nihon Kōki]]'' chronicle dated 813 AD, when it was recorded that the shrine was granted the rank of Junior 5th Rank, Lower Grade (従五位下). Per the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', it was promoted to Junior 4th Rank, Lower Grade in 861 AD and its name appears in the ''[[Engishiki]]'' records compiled in 927 AD as a major shrine of ancient [[Jōetsu region|Kubiki County]] in Echigo. In 1207 AD, the Buddhist prelate [[Shinran]] sought refuge at this shrine after he was exiled from [[Kyoto]] for his promotion of [[Pure Land Buddhism]]. During the [[Muromachi period]], the shrine was patronized by the powerful [[Uesugi clan]] and was named the ''ichinomiya'' of Echigo Province notwithstanding the fact that the [[Yahiko Shrine]] had an existing and much older claim to that title.[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 2]{{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130517061440/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf |date=2013-05-17 }}; retrieved 2012-3-13. During the [[Sengoku period]], the shrine was burned down in 1533 during battle between rival factions of the Uesugi clan and was rebuilt by [[Nagao Tamekage]] only to be destroyed again during the [[Siege of Otate]] in 1578. It was granted a fief of 13 ''[[koku]]'' in 1599 for its upkeep, which the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] increased to 100 ''koku'' in 1611. Following the [[Meiji restoration]], with the establishment of [[State Shinto]] in 1872, the shrine was originally designated as a "county shrine", but was later raised in status to that of a prefectural shrine (県社) in 1873. Due to [[coastal erosion]], the shrine was relocated about one kilometer inland to its present location in 1879. The shrine burned down in 1902, and a temporary structure was built in 1907, which was only replaced with a new shrine building in 2008.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is located about five minutes by car from [[Naoetsu Station]] on the [[JR East]] [[Shin'etsu Main Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery==
