旧{{jalink|県社}} |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|5月3日|Q2547}} |神事 = |地図 = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=居多神社|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|境内入り口}}]] '''居多神社'''（こたじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|新潟県|Q132705}}{{jalink|上越市|Q582289}}五智にある神社。{{jalink|式内社}}、{{jalink|越後国|Q666514}}{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|県社}}。 === 社名 === 社名「居多」は、『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}を始め諸文献で見られる古くからの表記である。訓について『神祇志料』では「ケタ」、九条家本・武田家本・吉田家本では「ヰタ」、『{{jalink|神名帳考証|Q11588824}}』では「コタ」と見えるが、現在では「こた」と読んでいる{{Sfn|越佐の神社|2002年|p=14-15}}。日本海沿岸に分布する{{jalink|気多神社 (曖昧さ回避)|Q11548075|気多神社}}の一社として古代には「けた」と呼ばれたとされ、「こた」「ゐた」と呼ばれるのは後世になってからと考えられている{{Sfn|越佐の神社|2002年|p=14-15}}{{Sfn|中世諸国一宮制|2000年|p=368-369}}。 なお「居」を「け」と読ませる古例では、古くは5世紀末の{{jalink|金錯銘鉄剣||稲荷山古墳出土鉄剣}}の「獲居（わけ）」の銘がある{{Sfn|中世諸国一宮制|2000年|p=368-369}}。 === 祭神 === 現在の祭神は次の4柱{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}{{Sfn|越佐の神社|2002年|p=14-15}}境内説明板では、祭神を大国主命・奴奈川姫命・建御名方命の3柱と記すほか、明治16年（1883年）の神社明細帳では大国主命・奴奈川姫命・事代主命の3柱とする {{Harv|中世諸国一宮制|2000年|p=368-369}}。。 * {{jalink|大国主|Q276944|大国主神}}（おおくにぬしのかみ） * {{jalink|沼河比売|Q11142832|奴奈川姫神}}（ぬながわひめのかみ） - 文献では「沼河比売」とも。大国主神の妻。 * {{jalink|建御名方神}}（たけみなかたのかみ） - 大国主神の子。 * {{jalink|事代主|Q8009611|事代主命神}}（ことしろぬしのみかみ） - 大国主神の子。 === 歴史 === === 創建 === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[気多大社|氣多大社]]（{{jalink|石川県|Q131281}}{{jalink|羽咋市|Q822585}}）}}{{small|{{jalink|能登国|Q1047671}}{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}。気多神社の総本社か。}}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|気多神社の分布}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} 創建は不詳。『頸城郡延喜式神社考』では神代の古跡であると伝える{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。社地は、{{jalink|慶応|Q776775}}2年（{{jalink|1866年|Q7712}}）まで現社地の北西約1キロメートルの身輪山（みのわやま）に存在した{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。 前述（「{{jalink|#社名||社名}}」節）のように、居多神社は「気多神」を祀る神社と考えられており、{{jalink|兵庫県|Q130290}}北部から{{jalink|新潟県|Q132705}}にかけての日本海側に分布する{{jalink|気多神社 (曖昧さ回避)|Q11548075|気多神社}}の一社とされる（「{{jalink|気多大社#気多苗裔神|Q11129340}}」を参照）。この気多神は出雲の人々に信奉された神とされており、『{{jalink|古事記|Q813031}}』での{{jalink|大国主|Q276944|大国主神}}による高志国（{{jalink|越国|Q1351611}}；現在の北陸地方）の{{jalink|奴奈川姫||奴奈川姫命神}}への妻問い説話と同様に、気多神の分布は出雲勢力の北陸地方への進出を物語ると考えられている{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。 === 概史 === 神社所蔵文書（伝『{{jalink|日本後紀|Q1077029}}』逸文）では{{jalink|弘仁|Q1373070}}4年（{{jalink|813年|Q30360}}）に「居多神」の{{jalink|神階|Q11591025}}が無位から従五位下に昇叙されたといい、その後神階は{{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}3年（{{jalink|861年|Q23913}}）に従四位下に昇叙された{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。 {{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}では、{{jalink|越後国|Q666514}}{{jalink|頸城郡|Q11664982}}に「居多神社」と記載され、{{jalink|式内社}}に列している{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。また、『{{jalink|朝野群載|Q11517735}}』の{{jalink|承暦|Q279743}}4年（{{jalink|1080年|Q19448}}）6月10日記事では「気多神」として記載が見えるほか{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}{{Sfn|中世諸国一宮制|2000年|p=368-369}}、社伝では{{jalink|承元|Q1194389}}元年（{{jalink|1207年|Q5232}}）に{{jalink|親鸞|Q512934}}が越後国府に流罪になった際には居多神社に参詣したという{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。 {{jalink|南北朝時代 (日本)|Q264012|南北朝時代}}以降は、居多神社は守護{{jalink|上杉氏|Q1150058|上杉家}}からの崇敬を受けて{{jalink|越後国|Q666514}}の{{jalink|一宮|Q1656379}}に位置づけられたとされる{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。居多神社を一宮とする史料は、{{jalink|貞和|Q1146143}}3年（{{jalink|1347年|Q6082}}）の居多神社所蔵文書を初見として、他の居多神社文書や上杉家文書等にも散見される{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。一方、越後国の一宮として知られる神社には{{jalink|弥彦村|Q1349667}}の{{jalink|彌彦神社|Q195684}}がある{{Sfn|中世諸国一宮制|2000年|p=365}}。これら越後国の一宮制の展開の詳細は明らかでないが、平安期までは「一宮」の呼称自体は使用していなかったものの彌彦神社が実質的な一宮であったと見て、南北朝期に居多神社が上杉家の崇敬を得て一宮を公称するとともに、彌彦神社側でも一宮を称し始めたとする説がある{{Sfn|中世諸国一宮制|2000年|p=365}}。 貞和3年（1347年）には幕府から修繕費として田井保が与えられたほか、{{jalink|観応|Q1145053}}2年）（{{jalink|1351年|Q6104}}）には越後守護・{{jalink|上杉憲顕|Q11359188}}から荒蒔保が寄進された{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。また{{jalink|文明 (日本)|Q1193544|文明}}18年（{{jalink|1486年|Q6646}}）には京都常光院の{{jalink|尭恵|Q11464814}}が（『北国紀行』）、{{jalink|長享|Q968843}}2年（{{jalink|1488年|Q4853}}）には京都{{jalink|相国寺|Q1786709}}僧の{{jalink|万里集九|Q11353943}}が居多神社に参詣している（『梅花無尽蔵』）{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。『北国紀行』によると「ながさき（花ヶ前）」という神主が社務を担ったとあり、その言では居多神が{{jalink|神功皇后|Q232803}}の{{jalink|三韓征伐|Q11249435}}の頃から北海擁護の神としてあらたかであるとしている{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。この神主の花ヶ前氏は任用国司として越後国に下った有力在庁であったとされ{{Sfn|中世諸国一宮制|2000年|p=365}}、現在まで神職を継承している。 {{jalink|戦国時代 (日本)|Q204023|戦国時代}}には戦乱に巻き込まれており、{{jalink|天文 (元号)|Q1192853|天文}}2年（{{jalink|1533年|Q6443}}）に{{jalink|上杉定憲}}によって社殿が焼かれ、同年に守護代・{{jalink|長尾為景|Q909963}}は上杉定憲ら討滅を祈願して社殿再興を約した{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。また{{jalink|上杉謙信|Q311080}}の死後、{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}6年（{{jalink|1578年|Q6707}}）から始まった後継者争い（{{jalink|御館の乱|Q7510546}}）では{{jalink|上杉景虎|Q1190934}}方についたため{{jalink|上杉景勝|Q1376605}}方の攻撃を受けて社殿を焼失した{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。この時に神官の花ヶ前盛貞・家盛父子は国外に逃亡し、盛貞は国外で死去したが、家盛は景勝の会津転封後に{{jalink|堀秀治|Q9755540}}が{{jalink|春日山城|Q1197131}}に入った際に帰国した{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。 {{jalink|慶長|Q1328185}}4年（{{jalink|1599年|Q6803}}）には堀秀治から社領として13石が与えられ、{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}に入ってから慶長16年（{{jalink|1611年|Q6768}}）には幕府から朱印地として100石が与えられた{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。 {{jalink|慶応|Q776775}}2年（{{jalink|1866年|Q7712}}）には身輪山の社地が{{jalink|海岸侵食|Q1542312}}によって崩壊し{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}、神霊は宮司宅に遷座し奉斎された{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}。{{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}後、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}5年（{{jalink|1872年|Q7753}}）5月に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}において{{jalink|郷社|Q134917316}}に列し、明治6年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）5月に{{jalink|県社}}に昇格した{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}。明治12年（{{jalink|1879年|Q7783}}）に現在地に社殿が造営され遷座した{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}。この社殿は明治35年（{{jalink|1902年|Q2043}}）に火災で焼失、明治40年（{{jalink|1907年|Q2048}}）に仮社殿が造営された{{Sfn|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}。その後、{{jalink|平成|Q234510}}20年（{{jalink|2008年|Q2004}}）6月に本社殿が造営されて現在に至っている。 === 神階 === {{jalink|六国史|Q1151168}}における神階奉叙の記録。表記はいずれも「居多神」。 * {{jalink|弘仁|Q1373070}}4年（{{jalink|813年|Q30360}}）7月、無位から従五位下 （居多神社蔵『神祇伯袖判居多神社位記証状』所収『{{jalink|日本後紀|Q1077029}}』逸文）居多神社文書に「日本後紀曰」として見える記事。『日本後紀』では弘仁4年7月条は欠失しており、貞観3年記事と照らし合わせても記事内容の信憑性は高いとされる {{Harv|居多神社（平凡社）|1986年}}{{Harv|中世諸国一宮制|2000年|p=368-369}}。 * {{jalink|貞観 (日本)|Q1075635|貞観}}3年（{{jalink|861年|Q23913}}）8月3日、従五位上から従四位下 （『{{jalink|日本三代実録|Q1052981}}』） === 境内 === 社地は{{jalink|慶応|Q776775}}2年（{{jalink|1866年|Q7712}}）までは現社地北西約1キロメートルにある身輪山（みのわやま）にあったが、明治12年（{{jalink|1879年|Q7783}}）に現在地に移転した{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。現在の社殿は{{jalink|平成|Q234510}}20年（{{jalink|2008年|Q2004}}）6月の造営。 境内には、葉が片方にのみ生える{{jalink|ヨシ|Q28557|芦}}「片葉の芦」が群生する{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。伝承では、親鸞が居多神社に参拝して祈願をすると境内の芦が一夜にして片葉になったという{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。この片葉の芦は「{{jalink|越後七不思議|Q11636487}}」の1つにも数えられている{{Sfn|居多神社（神々）|1985年}}。 祭神：{{jalink|ウカノミタマ|Q3080728|倉稲魂命}} なお慶応2年（1866年）までの旧社地では、境内社として天神社・八幡社・稲荷社・八坂社・諏訪社・大間神社の6社が祀られていた{{Sfn|明治神社誌料|1912年}}。 File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) Karita-jinja.JPG|雁田神社 File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|May 3rd|Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}, and {{jalink|modern company rank system|Q712534|old company rank}} is {{jalink|prefectural company}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' and {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{jalink|Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{jalink|kin-inscribed iron sword||iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{jalink|Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{jalink|Takeminakata no kami}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{jalink|Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{jalink|Noto Country|Q1047671}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{jalink|#company name||company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}} to {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{jalink|Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{jalink|Ekoku|Q13516 11}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{jalink|Nunagawa Hime||Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} ({{jalink|813 It is said that in the year |Q30360}}, the {{jalink|jinka|Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Kubiki District|Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{jalink|Asano Gunsai|Q11517735}}" in {{jalink|Joreki|Q279743}}4 ({{jalink|1080| Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{jalink|Jogen|Q1194389}} ({{jalink|1207|Q5232}}) It is said that when {{jalink|Shinran|Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{jalink|Uesugi clan|Q1150058|Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{jalink|1347|Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{jalink|Yahiko Shrine|Q195684}} in {{jalink|Yahiko Village|Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{jalink|Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{jalink|Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{jalink|1486|Q6646}}), {{jalink|Yakae|Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{jalink|Banri Shuku|Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{jalink|Shokokuji|Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}}'s {{jalink|Sankan Conquest|Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{jalink|astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{jalink|1533|Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{jalink|Sadanori Uesugi}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{jalink|Tamekage Nagao|Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{jalink|Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{jalink|Otate Rebellion|Q7 510546}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{jalink|Hideharu Hori|Q9755540}} entered {{jalink|Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{jalink|Edo period|Q18 4963}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{jalink|1611|Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{jalink|coastal erosion|Q15 42312}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in May of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{jalink|Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1879|Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{jalink|1902|Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{jalink|1907|Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{jalink|813|Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{jalink|Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jogan}}3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}20 ({{jalink|2008|Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{jalink|reeds|Q28557|reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{jalink|Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Single-leaf reed.JPG|Single-leaf reed File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Ichino Torii === Setsusha === * Karita Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Kōnosūreishin}}, {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Shinsōsōreishin}}Precinct explanation board. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Kurainatama-no-Mikoto}} Until 1866, six shrines were enshrined within the precincts of the former shrine: Tenjin Shrine, Yawata Shrine, Inari Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Suwa Shrine, and Oma Shrine. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) Karita-jinja.JPG|Kota-jinja Shrine File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) sekishi.JPG|Ishiji === Festival === Main festivals held at Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. * Annual festival (May 3rd) * Grand Festival (August 19th and 20th) * Niinamesai (November 23rd) === Cultural assets === === Joetsu City designated cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Wooden guardian dog (sculpture) [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/cultural-property/cultural-property-city038.html Wooden guardian dog] (Joetsu City homepage). ** Ita Shrine Documents (Old Documents) [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/cultural-property/cultural-property-city006.html Ita Shrine Documents] (Joetsu City homepage). - Medieval and early modern documents handed down at Ita Shrine. === Appeared works === * {{jalink|Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} 18 years ({{jalink|1486|Q6646}}), a poem that {{jalink|Takae|Q11464814}} recited when he visited Ita Shrine ("Hokukoku Kiko") {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}} *: {{Cquote|The heavenly fields, far beyond the clouds, Yashima Moru, gods and coolness, Okitsushihofu|20px||{{jalink|Yae|Q11464814}}, "Hokkaido Travelogue"}} === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}Gochi 6-1-11 '''Access''' * Railway: {{jalink|East Japan Railway|Q499071}} (JR East) {{jalink|Shin'etsu Main Line|Q1038051}} ・{{jalink|Echigo Tokimeki Railway|Q11260966}} {{jalink|Naoetsu Station|Q130065}} (about 25 minutes walk) '''Surroundings''' * {{jalink|Gochi Kokubunji|Q11372444}} - Successor temple of Echigo Kokubunji. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Ita Shrine|title={{jalink|Meiji Shrine Materials |Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/437}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 437-438. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1986|chapter=Ita Shrine|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 15 Place names in Niigata Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490158|ref={{Harvid|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor={{jalink|Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596}}|author=Moriaki Hanagamae|year=1985|chapter=Ita Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 8 Hokuriku|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022186|ref={{Harvid|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}}} - The author is the chief priest.* {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Medieval Countries Uninomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Research on Medieval Countries Uninomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Medieval Countries Uninomiya System|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=Moriaki Hanagamae|year=2002|chapter=Ita Shrine|title=Etsusa Shrine - Shikinaisha Rokujusan-|publisher=Niigata Nippo Business Company|isbn=978-4888629430|ref={{Harvid|Etsusa Shrine|2002}}}} - The author is the chief priest. === Related items === * {{jalink|Kidaisha|Q11129340|Kidaisha}} * Other shrines considered to be Echigo Province Ichinomiya ** {{jalink|Yahiko Shrine|Q195684}} ** {{jalink|Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|Amatsu Shrine}} === External link === {{Commonscat|Kota-jinja}} * [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/kanko/meisho-jo-07.html Ita Shrine] - Joetsu City Homepage * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/340202.html Ita Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto/Shrine Historical Materials Collection” {{Shinto horizontal}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kotashinsiya}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kota Shrine (Niigata)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kota Shrine (Niigata)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:ichinomiya]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Echigo Province]] [[Category:上越市の歴史]] [[Category:上越市の建築物]] [[Category:越後国の式内社]] [[Category:一宮]] [[Category:新潟県の旧県社]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:kkkkkkkkkkkkkk]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]] [[Category:nnnnnnnnn]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qq]]

== {{Shrine |Name = Ita Shrine |Image = [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) shaden.JPG|250px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}Gochi 6-1-11 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|37|9|59.18|N|138|13|23.41|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Okuninushi-kami}}
{{jalink|Numakawa-Hime|Q11142832|Numakawa-Hime}}
{{jalink|Takeminakata-kami}}
{{jalink|Kotoshiro-nushi}} |Divinity = |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
{{jalink|Echigokuni|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|May 3rd|Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}, and {{jalink|modern company rank system|Q712534|old company rank}} is {{jalink|prefectural company}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' and {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{jalink|Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{jalink|kin-inscribed iron sword||iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{jalink|Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{jalink|Takeminakata no kami}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{jalink|Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{jalink|Noto Country|Q1047671}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{jalink|#company name||company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}} to {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{jalink|Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{jalink|Ekoku|Q13516 11}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{jalink|Nunagawa Hime||Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} ({{jalink|813 It is said that in the year |Q30360}}, the {{jalink|jinka|Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Kubiki District|Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{jalink|Asano Gunsai|Q11517735}}" in {{jalink|Joreki|Q279743}}4 ({{jalink|1080| Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{jalink|Jogen|Q1194389}} ({{jalink|1207|Q5232}}) It is said that when {{jalink|Shinran|Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{jalink|Uesugi clan|Q1150058|Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{jalink|1347|Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{jalink|Yahiko Shrine|Q195684}} in {{jalink|Yahiko Village|Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{jalink|Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{jalink|Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{jalink|1486|Q6646}}), {{jalink|Yakae|Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{jalink|Banri Shuku|Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{jalink|Shokokuji|Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}}'s {{jalink|Sankan Conquest|Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{jalink|astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{jalink|1533|Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{jalink|Sadanori Uesugi}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{jalink|Tamekage Nagao|Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{jalink|Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{jalink|Otate Rebellion|Q7 510546}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{jalink|Hideharu Hori|Q9755540}} entered {{jalink|Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{jalink|Edo period|Q18 4963}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{jalink|1611|Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{jalink|coastal erosion|Q15 42312}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in May of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{jalink|Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1879|Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{jalink|1902|Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{jalink|1907|Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{jalink|813|Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{jalink|Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jogan}}3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}20 ({{jalink|2008|Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{jalink|reeds|Q28557|reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{jalink|Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Single-leaf reed.JPG|Single-leaf reed File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Ichino Torii === Setsusha === * Karita Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Kōnosūreishin}}, {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Shinsōsōreishin}}Precinct explanation board. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Kurainatama-no-Mikoto}} Until 1866, six shrines were enshrined within the precincts of the former shrine: Tenjin Shrine, Yawata Shrine, Inari Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Suwa Shrine, and Oma Shrine. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) Karita-jinja.JPG|Kota-jinja Shrine File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) sekishi.JPG|Ishiji === Festival === Main festivals held at Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. * Annual festival (May 3rd) * Grand Festival (August 19th and 20th) * Niinamesai (November 23rd) === Cultural assets === === Joetsu City designated cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Wooden guardian dog (sculpture) [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/cultural-property/cultural-property-city038.html Wooden guardian dog] (Joetsu City homepage). ** Ita Shrine Documents (Old Documents) [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/cultural-property/cultural-property-city006.html Ita Shrine Documents] (Joetsu City homepage). - Medieval and early modern documents handed down at Ita Shrine. === Appeared works === * {{jalink|Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} 18 years ({{jalink|1486|Q6646}}), a poem that {{jalink|Takae|Q11464814}} recited when he visited Ita Shrine ("Hokukoku Kiko") {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}} *: {{Cquote|The heavenly fields, far beyond the clouds, Yashima Moru, gods and coolness, Okitsushihofu|20px||{{jalink|Yae|Q11464814}}, "Hokkaido Travelogue"}} === Local information === '''Location''' * {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}Gochi 6-1-11 '''Access''' * Railway: {{jalink|East Japan Railway|Q499071}} (JR East) {{jalink|Shin'etsu Main Line|Q1038051}} ・{{jalink|Echigo Tokimeki Railway|Q11260966}} {{jalink|Naoetsu Station|Q130065}} (about 25 minutes walk) '''Surroundings''' * {{jalink|Gochi Kokubunji|Q11372444}} - Successor temple of Echigo Kokubunji. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Ita Shrine|title={{jalink|Meiji Shrine Materials |Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/437}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 437-438. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1986|chapter=Ita Shrine|title={{jalink|Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509}} 15 Place names in Niigata Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490158|ref={{Harvid|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor={{jalink|Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596}}|author=Moriaki Hanagamae|year=1985|chapter=Ita Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 8 Hokuriku|publisher={{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022186|ref={{Harvid|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}}} - The author is the chief priest.* {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Medieval Countries Uninomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Research on Medieval Countries Uninomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Medieval Countries Uninomiya System|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=Moriaki Hanagamae|year=2002|chapter=Ita Shrine|title=Etsusa Shrine - Shikinaisha Rokujusan-|publisher=Niigata Nippo Business Company|isbn=978-4888629430|ref={{Harvid|Etsusa Shrine|2002}}}} - The author is the chief priest. === Related items === * {{jalink|Kidaisha|Q11129340|Kidaisha}} * Other shrines considered to be Echigo Province Ichinomiya ** {{jalink|Yahiko Shrine|Q195684}} ** {{jalink|Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|Amatsu Shrine}} === External link === {{Commonscat|Kota-jinja}} * [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/kanko/meisho-jo-07.html Ita Shrine] - {{jalink|Echigokuni|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|May 3rd|Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}, and {{jalink|modern company rank system|Q712534|old company rank}} is {{jalink|prefectural company}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' and {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{jalink|Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{jalink|kin-inscribed iron sword||iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{jalink|Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{jalink|Takeminakata no kami}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{jalink|Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{jalink|Noto Country|Q1047671}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{jalink|#company name||company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}} to {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{jalink|Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{jalink|Ekoku|Q13516 11}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{jalink|Nunagawa Hime||Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} ({{jalink|813 It is said that in the year |Q30360}}, the {{jalink|jinka|Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Kubiki District|Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{jalink|Asano Gunsai|Q11517735}}" in {{jalink|Joreki|Q279743}}4 ({{jalink|1080| Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{jalink|Jogen|Q1194389}} ({{jalink|1207|Q5232}}) It is said that when {{jalink|Shinran|Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{jalink|Uesugi clan|Q1150058|Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{jalink|1347|Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{jalink|Yahiko Shrine|Q195684}} in {{jalink|Yahiko Village|Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{jalink|Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{jalink|Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{jalink|1486|Q6646}}), {{jalink|Yakae|Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{jalink|Banri Shuku|Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{jalink|Shokokuji|Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}}'s {{jalink|Sankan Conquest|Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{jalink|astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{jalink|1533|Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{jalink|Sadanori Uesugi}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{jalink|Tamekage Nagao|Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{jalink|Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{jalink|Otate Rebellion|Q7 510546}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{jalink|Hideharu Hori|Q9755540}} entered {{jalink|Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{jalink|Edo period|Q18 4963}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{jalink|1611|Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{jalink|coastal erosion|Q15 42312}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in May of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{jalink|Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1879|Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{jalink|1902|Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{jalink|1907|Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{jalink|813|Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{jalink|Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jogan}}3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}20 ({{jalink|2008|Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{jalink|reeds|Q28557|reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{jalink|Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}Gochi 6-1-11 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|37|9|59.18|N|138|13|23.41|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Okuninushi-kami}}

{{jalink|Kotoshiro-nushi}} |Divinity = |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
{{jalink|Echigokuni|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|May 3rd|Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}, and {{jalink|modern company rank system|Q712534|old company rank}} is {{jalink|prefectural company}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' and {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{jalink|Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{jalink|kin-inscribed iron sword||iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{jalink|Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{jalink|Takeminakata no kami}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{jalink|Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{jalink|Noto Country|Q1047671}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{jalink|#company name||company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}} to {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{jalink|Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{jalink|Ekoku|Q13516 11}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{jalink|Nunagawa Hime||Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} ({{jalink|813 It is said that in the year |Q30360}}, the {{jalink|jinka|Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Kubiki District|Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{jalink|Asano Gunsai|Q11517735}}" in {{jalink|Joreki|Q279743}}4 ({{jalink|1080| Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{jalink|Jogen|Q1194389}} ({{jalink|1207|Q5232}}) It is said that when {{jalink|Shinran|Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{jalink|Uesugi clan|Q1150058|Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{jalink|1347|Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{jalink|Yahiko Shrine|Q195684}} in {{jalink|Yahiko Village|Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{jalink|Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{jalink|Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{jalink|1486|Q6646}}), {{jalink|Yakae|Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{jalink|Banri Shuku|Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{jalink|Shokokuji|Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}}'s {{jalink|Sankan Conquest|Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{jalink|astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{jalink|1533|Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{jalink|Sadanori Uesugi}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{jalink|Tamekage Nagao|Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{jalink|Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{jalink|Otate Rebellion|Q7 510546}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{jalink|Hideharu Hori|Q9755540}} entered {{jalink|Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{jalink|Edo period|Q18 4963}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{jalink|1611|Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{jalink|coastal erosion|Q15 42312}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in May of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{jalink|Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1879|Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{jalink|1902|Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{jalink|1907|Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{jalink|813|Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{jalink|Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jogan}}3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}20 ({{jalink|2008|Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{jalink|reeds|Q28557|reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{jalink|Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Single-leaf reed.JPG|Single-leaf reed File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Ichino Torii === Setsusha === * Karita Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Kōnosūreishin}}, {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Shinsōsōreishin}}Precinct explanation board. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Kurainatama-no-Mikoto}} Until 1866, six shrines were enshrined within the precincts of the former shrine: Tenjin Shrine, Yawata Shrine, Inari Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Suwa Shrine, and Oma Shrine. House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}Gochi 6-1-11 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|37|9|59.18|N|138|13|23.41|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Okuninushi-kami}}
