{{jalink|Kotoshiro-nushi}} |Divinity = |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
{{jalink|Echigokuni|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}
Old {{jalink|Prefectural company}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|May 3rd|Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}{{jalink|Joetsu City|Q582289}}. {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}, and {{jalink|modern company rank system|Q712534|old company rank}} is {{jalink|prefectural company}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' and {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{jalink|Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{jalink|kin-inscribed iron sword||iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{jalink|Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{jalink|Takeminakata no kami}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{jalink|Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{jalink|Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{jalink|Noto Country|Q1047671}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{jalink|#company name||company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}} to {{jalink|Niigata Prefecture|Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{jalink|Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{jalink|Ekoku|Q13516 11}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{jalink|Nunagawa Hime||Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} ({{jalink|813 It is said that in the year |Q30360}}, the {{jalink|jinka|Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{jalink|extension (Era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" {{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{jalink|Echigo Province|Q666514}}{{jalink|Kubiki District|Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{jalink|Shikiuchi}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{jalink|Asano Gunsai|Q11517735}}" in {{jalink|Joreki|Q279743}}4 ({{jalink|1080| Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{jalink|Jogen|Q1194389}} ({{jalink|1207|Q5232}}) It is said that when {{jalink|Shinran|Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{jalink|Uesugi clan|Q1150058|Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{jalink|1347|Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{jalink|Yahiko Shrine|Q195684}} in {{jalink|Yahiko Village|Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{jalink|Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{jalink|Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{jalink|1486|Q6646}}), {{jalink|Yakae|Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{jalink|Banri Shuku|Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{jalink|Shokokuji|Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{jalink|Empress Jingu|Q232803}}'s {{jalink|Sankan Conquest|Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{jalink|Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{jalink|astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{jalink|1533|Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{jalink|Sadanori Uesugi}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{jalink|Tamekage Nagao|Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{jalink|Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{jalink|Otate Rebellion|Q7 510546}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{jalink|Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{jalink|Hideharu Hori|Q9755540}} entered {{jalink|Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keicho|Q1328185}}4 ({{jalink|1599|Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{jalink|Edo period|Q18 4963}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{jalink|1611|Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{jalink|coastal erosion|Q15 42312}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in May of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}5 ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), {{jalink|Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534}} It was ranked as {{jalink|gosha|Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{jalink|prefectural shrine}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1879|Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{jalink|1902|Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{jalink|1907|Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{jalink|Rokukokushi|Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{jalink|Honin|Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{jalink|813|Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{jalink|Nihon Koki|Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{jalink|Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|Jogan}}3rd year ({{jalink|861|Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{jalink|Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{jalink|Keio|Q776775}}2 ({{jalink|1866|Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}20 ({{jalink|2008|Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{jalink|reeds|Q28557|reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{jalink|Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Single-leaf reed.JPG|Single-leaf reed File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Ichino Torii === Setsusha === * Karita Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Kōnosūreishin}}, {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Shinsōsōreishin}}Precinct explanation board. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Kurainatama-no-Mikoto}} Until 1866, six shrines were enshrined within the precincts of the former shrine: Tenjin Shrine, Yawata Shrine, Inari Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Suwa Shrine, and Oma Shrine. Old {{ill||lt=Prefectural company|UNKNOWN}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=May 3rd|Q2547|WD=Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Joetsu City|Q582289|WD=Q582289}}. {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}, and {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank}} is {{ill||lt=prefectural company|UNKNOWN}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' and {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{ill|lt=Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{ill|lt=kin-inscribed iron sword||WD=|lt=iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{ill|lt=Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{ill||lt=Takeminakata no kami|UNKNOWN}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{ill|lt=Noto Country|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{ill|lt=#company name||WD=|lt=company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}} to {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{ill|lt=Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340|WD=Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{ill||lt=Ekoku|UNKNOWN}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{ill|lt=Nunagawa Hime||WD=|lt=Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} ({{ill|lt=813 It is said that in the year|Q30360|WD=Q30360}}, the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Kubiki District|Q11664982|WD=Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{ill||lt=Shikiuchi|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{ill|lt=Asano Gunsai|Q11517735|WD=Q11517735}}" in {{ill|lt=Joreki|Q279743|WD=Q279743}}4 ({{ill|lt=1080|Q19448|WD=Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{ill|lt=Jogen|Q1194389|WD=Q1194389}} ({{ill|lt=1207|Q5232|WD=Q5232}}) It is said that when {{ill|lt=Shinran|Q512934|WD=Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{ill|lt=Uesugi clan|Q1150058|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1347|Q6082|WD=Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{ill|lt=Yahiko Shrine|Q195684|WD=Q195684}} in {{ill|lt=Yahiko Village|Q1349667|WD=Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{ill|lt=Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188|WD=Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{ill|lt=Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646}}), {{ill|lt=Yakae|Q11464814|WD=Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{ill|lt=Banri Shuku|Q11353943|WD=Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{ill|lt=Shokokuji|Q1786709|WD=Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}}'s {{ill|lt=Sankan Conquest|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{ill|lt=astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{ill||lt=Sadanori Uesugi|UNKNOWN}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{ill|lt=Tamekage Nagao|Q909963|WD=Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080|WD=Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{ill||lt=Otate Rebellion|UNKNOWN}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934|WD=Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605|WD=Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{ill|lt=Hideharu Hori|Q9755540|WD=Q9755540}} entered {{ill|lt=Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131|WD=Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|WD=Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{ill||lt=Edo period|UNKNOWN}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{ill||lt=coastal erosion|UNKNOWN}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in May of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{ill||lt=prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1879|Q7783|WD=Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{ill|lt=813|Q30360|WD=Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}}3rd year ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}20 ({{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{ill|lt=reeds|Q28557|WD=Q28557|lt=reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{ill|lt=Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487|WD=Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Single-leaf reed.JPG|Single-leaf reed File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Ichino Torii === Setsusha === * Karita Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi||WD=|lt=Kōnosūreishin}}, {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi||WD=|lt=Shinsōsōreishin}}Precinct explanation board. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Kurainatama-no-Mikoto}} Until 1866, six shrines were enshrined within the precincts of the former shrine: Tenjin Shrine, Yawata Shrine, Inari Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Suwa Shrine, and Oma Shrine. Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|UNKNOWN}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=May 3rd|Q2547|WD=Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Joetsu City|Q582289|WD=Q582289}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}, and {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|UNKNOWN}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' and {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{ill|lt=Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{ill|lt=kin-inscribed iron sword||WD=|lt=iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{ill|lt=Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata no kami|UNKNOWN}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{ill|lt=Noto Country|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{ill|lt=#company name||WD=|lt=company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}} to {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{ill|lt=Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340|WD=Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ekoku|UNKNOWN}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{ill|lt=Nunagawa Hime||WD=|lt=Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} ({{ill|lt=813 It is said that in the year|Q30360|WD=Q30360}}, the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Kubiki District|Q11664982|WD=Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi|UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{ill|lt=Asano Gunsai|Q11517735|WD=Q11517735}}" in {{ill|lt=Joreki|Q279743|WD=Q279743}}4 ({{ill|lt=1080|Q19448|WD=Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{ill|lt=Jogen|Q1194389|WD=Q1194389}} ({{ill|lt=1207|Q5232|WD=Q5232}}) It is said that when {{ill|lt=Shinran|Q512934|WD=Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{ill|lt=Uesugi clan|Q1150058|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1347|Q6082|WD=Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{ill|lt=Yahiko Shrine|Q195684|WD=Q195684}} in {{ill|lt=Yahiko Village|Q1349667|WD=Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{ill|lt=Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188|WD=Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{ill|lt=Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646}}), {{ill|lt=Yakae|Q11464814|WD=Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{ill|lt=Banri Shuku|Q11353943|WD=Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{ill|lt=Shokokuji|Q1786709|WD=Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}}'s {{ill|lt=Sankan Conquest|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{ill|lt=astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sadanori Uesugi|UNKNOWN}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{ill|lt=Tamekage Nagao|Q909963|WD=Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080|WD=Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Otate Rebellion|UNKNOWN}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934|WD=Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605|WD=Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{ill|lt=Hideharu Hori|Q9755540|WD=Q9755540}} entered {{ill|lt=Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131|WD=Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|WD=Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|UNKNOWN}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=coastal erosion|UNKNOWN}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in May of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|UNKNOWN}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1879|Q7783|WD=Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{ill|lt=813|Q30360|WD=Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}}3rd year ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}20 ({{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{ill|lt=reeds|Q28557|WD=Q28557|lt=reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{ill|lt=Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487|WD=Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Single-leaf reed.JPG|Single-leaf reed File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Ichino Torii === Setsusha === * Karita Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi||WD=|lt=Kōnosūreishin}}, {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi||WD=|lt=Shinsōsōreishin}}Precinct explanation board. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Kurainatama-no-Mikoto}} Until 1866, six shrines were enshrined within the precincts of the former shrine: Tenjin Shrine, Yawata Shrine, Inari Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Suwa Shrine, and Oma Shrine. Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=May 3rd|Q2547|WD=Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Joetsu City|Q582289|WD=Q582289}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}, and {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' and {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{ill|lt=Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{ill|lt=kin-inscribed iron sword||WD=|lt=iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{ill|lt=Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata no kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{ill|lt=Noto Country|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{ill|lt=#company name||WD=|lt=company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}} to {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{ill|lt=Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340|WD=Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ekoku|1=UNKNOWN}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{ill|lt=Nunagawa Hime||WD=|lt=Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} ({{ill|lt=813 It is said that in the year|Q30360|WD=Q30360}}, the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Kubiki District|Q11664982|WD=Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{ill|lt=Asano Gunsai|Q11517735|WD=Q11517735}}" in {{ill|lt=Joreki|Q279743|WD=Q279743}}4 ({{ill|lt=1080|Q19448|WD=Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{ill|lt=Jogen|Q1194389|WD=Q1194389}} ({{ill|lt=1207|Q5232|WD=Q5232}}) It is said that when {{ill|lt=Shinran|Q512934|WD=Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{ill|lt=Uesugi clan|Q1150058|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1347|Q6082|WD=Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{ill|lt=Yahiko Shrine|Q195684|WD=Q195684}} in {{ill|lt=Yahiko Village|Q1349667|WD=Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{ill|lt=Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188|WD=Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{ill|lt=Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646}}), {{ill|lt=Yakae|Q11464814|WD=Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{ill|lt=Banri Shuku|Q11353943|WD=Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{ill|lt=Shokokuji|Q1786709|WD=Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}}'s {{ill|lt=Sankan Conquest|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{ill|lt=astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sadanori Uesugi|1=UNKNOWN}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{ill|lt=Tamekage Nagao|Q909963|WD=Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080|WD=Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Otate Rebellion|1=UNKNOWN}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934|WD=Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605|WD=Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{ill|lt=Hideharu Hori|Q9755540|WD=Q9755540}} entered {{ill|lt=Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131|WD=Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|WD=Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=coastal erosion|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in May of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1879|Q7783|WD=Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{ill|lt=813|Q30360|WD=Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}}3rd year ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}20 ({{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{ill|lt=reeds|Q28557|WD=Q28557|lt=reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{ill|lt=Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487|WD=Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Single-leaf reed.JPG|Single-leaf reed File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Ichino Torii === Setsusha === * Karita Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi||WD=|lt=Kōnosūreishin}}, {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi||WD=|lt=Shinsōsōreishin}}Precinct explanation board. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Kurainatama-no-Mikoto}} Until 1866, six shrines were enshrined within the precincts of the former shrine: Tenjin Shrine, Yawata Shrine, Inari Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Suwa Shrine, and Oma Shrine. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) Karita-jinja.JPG|Kota-jinja Shrine File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) sekishi.JPG|Ishiji === Festival === Main festivals held at Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. * Annual festival (May 3rd) * Grand Festival (August 19th and 20th) * Niinamesai (November 23rd) === Cultural assets === === Joetsu City designated cultural property === *Tangible cultural property ** Wooden guardian dog (sculpture) [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/cultural-property/cultural-property-city038.html Wooden guardian dog] (Joetsu City homepage). ** Ita Shrine Documents (Old Documents) [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/cultural-property/cultural-property-city006.html Ita Shrine Documents] (Joetsu City homepage). - Medieval and early modern documents handed down at Ita Shrine. === Appeared works === * {{ill|lt=Bunmei (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei}} 18 years ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646}}), a poem that {{ill|lt=Takae|Q11464814|WD=Q11464814}} recited when he visited Ita Shrine ("Hokukoku Kiko") {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}} *: {{Cquote|The heavenly fields, far beyond the clouds, Yashima Moru, gods and coolness, Okitsushihofu|20px||{{ill|lt=Yae|Q11464814|WD=Q11464814}}, "Hokkaido Travelogue"}} === Local information === '''Location''' * {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Joetsu City|Q582289|WD=Q582289}}Gochi 6-1-11 '''Access''' * Railway: {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway|Q499071|WD=Q499071}} (JR East) {{ill|lt=Shin'etsu Main Line|Q1038051|WD=Q1038051}} ・{{ill|lt=Echigo Tokimeki Railway|Q11260966|WD=Q11260966}} {{ill|lt=Naoetsu Station|Q130065|WD=Q130065}} (about 25 minutes walk) '''Surroundings''' * {{ill|lt=Gochi Kokubunji|Q11372444|WD=Q11372444}} - Successor temple of Echigo Kokubunji. === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} '''Notes''' {{reflist|group="Note"}} '''Source''' {{reflist}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Ita Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Materials|Q11486103|WD=Q11486103}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Materials Editorial Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Materials|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/437}} "Meiji Shrine Journal Materials Fuken Gosha Volume 1"] (National Diet Library Digital Collection) Reference frames 437-438. * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=|year=1986|chapter=Ita Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Japanese historical place name system|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 15 Place names in Niigata Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=4582490158|ref={{Harvid|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor={{ill|lt=Kenichi Tanigawa|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596}}|author=Moriaki Hanagamae|year=1985|chapter=Ita Shrine|title=Japanese Gods -Shrines and Sacred Sites- 8 Hokuriku|publisher={{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470}}|isbn=4560022186|ref={{Harvid|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}}} - The author is the chief priest.* {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=Medieval Countries Uninomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Research on Medieval Countries Uninomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Medieval Countries Uninomiya System|2000}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|editor=|author=Moriaki Hanagamae|year=2002|chapter=Ita Shrine|title=Etsusa Shrine - Shikinaisha Rokujusan-|publisher=Niigata Nippo Business Company|isbn=978-4888629430|ref={{Harvid|Etsusa Shrine|2002}}}} - The author is the chief priest. === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Kidaisha|Q11129340|WD=Q11129340|lt=Kidaisha}} * Other shrines considered to be Echigo Province Ichinomiya ** {{ill|lt=Yahiko Shrine|Q195684|WD=Q195684}} ** {{ill|lt=Amatsu Shrine (Itoigawa City)|Q172253|WD=Q172253|lt=Amatsu Shrine}} === External link === {{Commonscat|Kota-jinja}} * [http://www.city.joetsu.niigata.jp/site/kanko/meisho-jo-07.html Ita Shrine] - == {{Shrine |Name = Ita Shrine |Image = [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) shaden.JPG|250px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Joetsu City|Q582289|WD=Q582289}}Gochi 6-1-11 |Position = {{Wiki coordinates 2 degrees minutes, seconds|37|9|59.18|N|138|13|23.41|E|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Okuninushi-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|lt=Numakawa-Hime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Numakawa-Hime}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata-kami|1=UNKNOWN}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshiro-nushi|1=UNKNOWN}} |Divinity = |Company rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (small)
{{ill|lt=Echigokuni|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}
Old {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}} |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = |alias= |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=May 3rd|Q2547|WD=Q2547}} |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{location map|Japan Niigata|label=Ita Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|caption=|width=210|float=center}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=210}}}} [[File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) entrance.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Precinct entrance}}]] '''Kota Shrine'' (Kota Jinja) is a shrine located in Gochi, {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}{{ill|lt=Joetsu City|Q582289|WD=Q582289}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}, and {{ill|lt=modern company rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=old company rank}} is {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural company|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Company name === The company name ``Ita'' is an old notation that can be found in various documents including ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' and {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}}. Regarding Kun, it appears to be ``keta'' in the ``Jingishiryo'', ``eta'' in the Kujo family book, Takeda family book, and Yoshida family book, and ``kota'' in ``{{ill|lt=Jinmyocho kosho|Q11588824|WD=Q11588824}}'', but it is currently read as ``kota'' {{Sfn|Etsusa Shrine|2002|p=14-15}}. Keta Shrine located along the coast of the Sea of Japan (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said that it was called ``Keta'' in ancient times, and it is thought that it was not called ``Kota'' or ``Ita'' until later times. In addition, there is an ancient example of reading ``ke'' as ``ke'' on a {{ill|lt=kin-inscribed iron sword|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=iron sword excavated from the Inariyama Tumulus}} dating back to the end of the 5th century {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. === Enshrined God === The following four deities are currently enshrined. group="Note">In the explanation board in the precincts, the enshrined deities are listed as 3 deities: Okuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawahime no Mikoto, and Takeminakata no Mikoto, and in the shrine statement book of 1883, they are listed as 3 deities: Ōkuninushi no Mikoto, Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto, and Jishironushi no Mikoto. {{Harv|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=368-369}}. . * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} (Ookuninushi no Kami) * {{ill|lt=Numagawa Hime|Q11142832|WD=Q11142832|lt=Nunagawa Hime-kami}} (Nunagawa Himenokami) - Also known as ``Numakawa Hime'' in literature. Wife of Okuninushi. * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Takeminakata no kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Takeminakata no kami) - Son of Okuninushi no kami. * {{ill|lt=Kotoshironushi|Q8009611|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi no Kami}} (Kotoshironushi Mikami) - Son of Okuninushi. === History === === Founding === [[File:Keta-taisha keidai.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|[[Kitaisha|Kidaisha]]({{ill|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|Q131281|WD=Q131281}}{{ jalink|Hakui City|Q822585}})}}{{small|{{ill|lt=Noto Country|Q1047671|WD=Q1047671}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}}. Is it the main headquarters of Keta Shrine? }}]] {{Location map+|Japan|width=220|float=right|caption={{Center|Distribution of Keta Shrine}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=55|lat_sec=33.63|lon_deg=136|lon_min=46|lon_sec=2.79|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=14|lat_sec=22.05|lon_deg=137|lon_min=11|lon_sec=52.22|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=21|lat_sec=27.27|lon_deg=136|lon_min=24|lon_sec=26.05|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}}{{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=36|lat_min=48|lat_sec=0.80|lon_deg=137|lon_min=2|lon_sec=39.55|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=37|lat_min=9|lat_sec=59.18|lon_deg=138|lon_min=13|lon_sec=23.41|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} {{Location map~|Japan|lat_deg=35|lat_min=28|lat_sec=41.38|lon_deg=134|lon_min=47|lon_sec=35.68|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=7}} }} Founding date is unknown. "Kubiki-gun Engishiki Shrine Review" says that it is an ancient site from the age of the gods {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine site existed on Mt. Minowa, about 1 km northwest of the current shrine site, until {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.As mentioned above (section "{{ill|lt=#company name|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=company name}}"), Ita Shrine is thought to be a shrine that enshrines the god Keta, and is located on the Sea of Japan side from northern {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}} to {{ill|lt=Niigata Prefecture|Q132705|WD=Q132705}}. (Disambiguation)|Q11548075|Keta Shrine}} It is said to be one of the shrines (see "{{ill|lt=Keta Shrine #Keta Nae Descendant God|Q11129340|WD=Q11129340}}"). This Ketakami is said to be a god worshiped by the people of Izumo, and is said to be the god of Takashi Province ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ekoku|1=UNKNOWN}}; Similar to the story of the marriage of {{ill|lt=Nunagawa Hime|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Nunagawa Hime no Mikoto}} in the current Hokuriku region, the distribution of Ketakami is thought to tell the story of the advance of the Izumo force into the Hokuriku region.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. === Outline === According to the documents in the shrine's collection (according to the legend "{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}" Itbun), 4 years ago ({{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} ({{ill|lt=813 It is said that in the year|Q30360|WD=Q30360}}, the {{ill|lt=jinka|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025}} of ``Itagami'' was promoted from no rank to junior fifth rank, and after that, the divine rank was {{jalink|jogan}. (Japan)|Q1075635|Jokan}} Promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower in 3rd year ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.{{ill|lt=extension (Era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" {{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shinmyocho}} is described as "Ita Shrine" in {{ill|lt=Echigo Province|Q666514|WD=Q666514}}{{ill|lt=Kubiki District|Q11664982|WD=Q11664982}}, and is listed as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN}}{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, "{{ill|lt=Asano Gunsai|Q11517735|WD=Q11517735}}" in {{ill|lt=Joreki|Q279743|WD=Q279743}}4 ({{ill|lt=1080|Q19448|WD=Q19448}}) In the June 10th article, it appears to be listed as "Keta Shrine" and also {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}{{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries| 2000|p=368-369}}, according to the company biography, in the first year of {{ill|lt=Jogen|Q1194389|WD=Q1194389}} ({{ill|lt=1207|Q5232|WD=Q5232}}) It is said that when {{ill|lt=Shinran|Q512934|WD=Q512934}} was exiled to Echigo Kokufu, he visited Ita Shrine {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}.{{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=After the Nanbokucho period}}, Ida Shrine was revered by the guardian {{ill|lt=Uesugi clan|Q1150058|WD=Q1150058|lt=Uesugi family}}. {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} is said to have been located in {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379}} of k|Echigo Province|Q666514}}. The historical documents that identify Ita Shrine as a shrine were first seen in the documents held by Ita Shrine dated 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1347|Q6082|WD=Q6082}}), and are also found here and there in other Ita Shrine documents and Uesugi family documents.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. On the other hand, among the shrines known as Ichinomiya in Echigo Province, there is {{ill|lt=Yahiko Shrine|Q195684|WD=Q195684}} in {{ill|lt=Yahiko Village|Q1349667|WD=Q1349667}} {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}. The details of the development of the Ichinomiya system in Echigo are not clear, but it is believed that Yahiko Shrine was actually an Ichinomiya shrine, although the name "Ichinomiya" itself was not used until the Heian period. There is a theory that Niita Shrine gained the reverence of the Uesugi family and officially called itself Ichinomiya, and Yahiko Shrine also began to call it Ichinomiya {{Sfn|Ichinomiya System in Medieval Countries|2000|p=365}}.In the 3rd year of Teiwa (1347), the shogunate granted Taiho for repair costs, and in 1351 |Q6104}}) Aramakiho was donated by Echigo Shugo {{ill|lt=Noriaki Uesugi|Q11359188|WD=Q11359188}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also {{ill|lt=Civilization (Japan)|Q1193544|WD=Q1193544|lt=Bunmei}} In the 18th year ({{ill|lt=1486|Q6646|WD=Q6646}}), {{ill|lt=Yakae|Q11464814|WD=Q11464814}} of Joko-in Temple in Kyoto ("Northern Travels"), Ink|1488|Q4853}}), {{ill|lt=Banri Shuku|Q11353943|WD=Q11353943}}, a monk in Kyoto {{ill|lt=Shokokuji|Q1786709|WD=Q1786709}}, visits Ita Shrine (``Baika Mujinzo'') {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. According to "Hokugoku Kiko," a priest named ``Nagasaki (Hanaga-mae)'' was in charge of the shrine's affairs, and it says that Ita-jinja became a new deity to protect the North Sea from the time of {{ill|lt=Empress Jingu|Q232803|WD=Q232803}}'s {{ill|lt=Sankan Conquest|Q11249435|WD=Q11249435}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The Hanagamae clan, the priest of this shrine, is said to have been an influential zaisho who descended to Echigo Province as appointed Kokushi {{Sfn|Ichinomiya system in medieval countries|2000|p=365}}, and has continued to hold the Shinto priesthood to this day.{{ill|lt=Sengoku period (Japan)|Q204023|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, it was involved in wars, and {{ill|lt=astronomical (era name)|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun}} In 2nd year ({{ill|lt=1533|Q6443|WD=Q6443}}), the shrine building was burnt down by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sadanori Uesugi|1=UNKNOWN}}, and in the same year In 1986, the Shugodai {{ill|lt=Tamekage Nagao|Q909963|WD=Q909963}} prayed for the destruction of Sadanori Uesugi and others and promised to rebuild the shrine.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Also, after the death of {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|Q311080|WD=Q311080}}, a succession dispute ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Otate Rebellion|1=UNKNOWN}}), the shrine was attacked by the {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kagetora|Q1190934|WD=Q1190934}} side and was attacked by the {{ill|lt=Uesugi Kagetora|Q1376605|WD=Q1376605}} side, and the shrine building was burned down.{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. At this time, the priest Hanagamae Morisada and Iemori father and son fled abroad, and Morisada died abroad, but Iemori returned to Japan after Kagekatsu's transfer to Aizu and when {{ill|lt=Hideharu Hori|Q9755540|WD=Q9755540}} entered {{ill|lt=Kasugayama Castle|Q1197131|WD=Q1197131}} {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}}4 ({{ill|lt=1599|Q6803|WD=Q6803}}), Hideharu Hori granted 13 koku as shrine territory, and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Edo period|1=UNKNOWN}} In the 16th year of the Keicho era ({{ill|lt=1611|Q6768|WD=Q6768}}), the shogunate granted 100 koku as a red stamp land {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}.In {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}), the shrine grounds of Mt.Minowa were {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=coastal erosion|1=UNKNOWN}}, it was destroyed by {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and the divine spirit was transferred to the chief priest's house and enshrined there {{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Journal |1912}}. After {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in May of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}5 ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), {{ill|lt=Modern Company Ranking System|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} It was ranked as {{ill|lt=gosha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} in May 1873, and was promoted to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} in May 1873|1912}}. In the 12th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1879|Q7783|WD=Q7783}}), a shrine building was built at the current location and the shrine was relocated{{Sfn|Meiji Shrine Archives|1912}}. This shrine building was destroyed by fire in 1902 ({{ill|lt=1902|Q2043|WD=Q2043}}), and a temporary shrine building was built in 40th year of Meiji ({{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|WD=Q2048}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. After that, the main building was built in June 2008 ({{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}) and remains there today. === God level === Record of divine investiture in {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|WD=Q1151168}}. Both are written as ``Itagami''. * {{ill|lt=Honin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} July 4th year ({{ill|lt=813|Q30360|WD=Q30360}}), from no rank to junior fifth rank (an excerpt from ``{{ill|lt=Nihon Koki|Q1077029|WD=Q1077029}}'' from the ``Jingi Hakusodehan Iita Shrine Iki Certificate'' owned by Ida Shrine) Articles that appear to be "Nihon Koki" in Ita Shrine documents. In "Nihon Koki," the article written in July of the 4th year of Konin is missing, and the credibility of the article is said to be high even when compared with the article of the 3rd year of Jogan. * {{ill|lt=Jogan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jogan}}3rd year ({{ill|lt=861|Q23913|WD=Q23913}}) August 3rd, from the top of the fifth rank to the bottom of the fourth rank (``{{ill|lt=Japan Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}'') === Precincts === Until {{ill|lt=Keio|Q776775|WD=Q776775}}2 ({{ill|lt=1866|Q7712|WD=Q7712}}), the company site was Mt. Minowayama), but moved to its current location in 1879 (1879|Q7783}}) {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. The current shrine building was constructed in June of {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}}20 ({{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|WD=Q2004}}). In the precincts, {{ill|lt=reeds|Q28557|WD=Q28557|lt=reeds}} ``single-leaf reeds'' grow in clusters {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. Legend has it that when Shinran visited Ita Shrine and prayed, the reeds in the shrine grounds became single leaves overnight {{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. This single-leaf reed is also counted as one of the "{{ill|lt=Echigo Seven Wonders|Q11636487|WD=Q11636487}}"{{Sfn|Ita Shrine (Gods)|1985}}. File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) honden.JPG|Main shrine File:Single-leaf reed.JPG|Single-leaf reed File:Kota-jinja (Joetsu) torii.JPG|Ichino Torii === Setsusha === * Karita Shrine - Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Kōnosūreishin}}, {{ill|lt=Kamimusubi|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=Shinsōsōreishin}}Precinct explanation board. * Inari Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Kurainatama-no-Mikoto}} Until 1866, six shrines were enshrined within the precincts of the former shrine: Tenjin Shrine, Yawata Shrine, Inari Shrine, Yasaka Shrine, Suwa Shrine, and Oma Shrine.
